message.content:
## Web Searcher: voice control — cloning, prosody, multi-speaker

Research question: "BEST LOCAL / OPEN-SOURCE TEXT-TO-SPEECH (TTS) MODELS TO REPLACE OR AUGMENT KOKORO for an English educational-podcast pipeline, as of 2026. Baseline to beat: Kokoro-82M (hexgrad/Kokoro-82M), currently run locally on Apple Silicon (CPU + MPS), producing 24kHz audio, multiple voices (af_heart/am_onyx/bf_isabella/bm_george etc.), American + British English, two-voice format (narrator + question voice). Evaluate candidates across these angles: (1) QUALITY/NATURALNESS — which open/local TTS models beat Kokoro on naturalness and intelligibility for English (candidates to assess: XTTS-v2/Coqui, Piper, StyleTTS2, F5-TTS, Fish-Speech/OpenAudio, Parler-TTS, MeloTTS, Orpheus-TTS, Chatterbox, Kitten-TTS, Zonos, and any newer 2025-2026 open models) — report MOS/quality benchmarks, license (commercial/redistribution OK?), and model size. (2) WORD/PHONEME-LEVEL TIMING & ALIGNMENT — which models natively output word/phoneme timestamps (durations), and what forced-alignment tools (Montreal Forced Aligner, WhisperX, aeneas, ctc-forced-aligner) best produce a text↔audio timing map from existing audio — this enables content-aware speed compression, script-highlighting, and a true text-to-audio map. (3) HIGH-SPEED COMPREHENSIBILITY — which model's output stays most intelligible under aggressive time-compression / at high playback speed (crisp consonants, clean formant transitions); also the best FORMANT/robotic option (eSpeak-NG, RHVoice, DECtalk-like) for an extreme 16x 'ultra' track. (4) MULTI-VOICE & VOICE CONTROL — voice variety, voice cloning, prosody/rate control, and ease of adding new voices for a narrator+question two-speaker format. (5) LOCAL-INFERENCE PRACTICALITY on Apple Silicon and CPU — real-time factor / batch render speed, RAM/VRAM needs, MPS/CoreML/ONNX support, latency, and integration effort to swap into a Python pipeline that today calls Kokoro. For EACH recommended model give: quality vs Kokoro, license, local-inference feasibility on a Mac, whether it emits alignment, and a clear verdict on whether it's worth switching to (or adding alongside Kokoro). Prefer authoritative/benchmark sources and recent (2025-2026) comparisons; flag where claims are vendor marketing vs independent evaluation."

Your angle: **voice control — cloning, prosody, multi-speaker** — Evaluates voice variety, cloning, prosody/rate control, and ease of adding voices for a narrator+question two-speaker format (Angle 4).
Search query: `open source TTS voice cloning prosody rate control multi-speaker add custom voice XTTS-v2 Chatterbox Fish-Speech Zonos two-speaker narrator quality`

## Task
Use WebSearch with the query above (or a refined version). Return the top 4-6 most relevant results.
Rank by relevance to the ORIGINAL question, not just the search query. Skip obvious SEO spam/content farms.
Include a short snippet capturing why each result is relevant.

Structured output only.

- agents-sdk: Build AI agents on Cloudflare Workers using the Agents SDK. Biases towards retrieval from Cloudflare docs over pre-trained knowledge.
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.

BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

Links: [{"title":"From Qwen3-TTS to Chatterbox - Finally Getting Voice Cloning Right","url":"https://www.archy.net/from-qwen3-tts-to-chatterbox-finally-getting-voice-cloning-right/"},{"title":"Chatterbox: The Leading Family of Open-Source AI Voice Models That’s Changing the Game","url":"https://medium.com/@vinodpolinati/chatterbox-the-leading-family-of-open-source-ai-voice-models-thats-changing-the-game-150991d5cf8d"},{"title":"Chatterbox TTS Review: The Open-Source Voice Revolution ...","url":"https://reviewnexa.com/chatterbox-tts-review/"},{"title":"Calliope: A TTS-based Narrated E-book Creator Ensuring Exact Synchronization, Privacy, and Layout Fidelity","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2602.10735"},{"title":"Chatterbox AI — Real-Time Voice Cloning & Text-to-Speech Generator | Online TTS","url":"https://chatterboxai.net/"},{"title":"TTS Tools","url":"https://docs.aihub.gg/tts/tts-tools/"},{"title":"ClaritySpeech: Dementia Obfuscation in Speech","url":"https://arxiv.org/pdf/2507.09282"},{"title":"ResembleAI/chatterbox · Hugging Face","url":"https://huggingface.co/ResembleAI/chatterbox"},{"title":"GitHub - resemble-ai/chatterbox: SoTA open-source TTS · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/resemble-ai/chatterbox"}]

Based on the search results, here's a comprehensive comparison of Chatterbox TTS and XTTS-v2:

## Architecture & Design

XTTS-v2 is an autoregressive transformer model based on a GPT-2 architecture that predicts discrete audio tokens conditioned on input text and a reference speaker embedding, which are then converted into a continuous waveform using a HiFi-GAN-based decoder. In contrast, Chatterbox employs a non-autoregressive Flow Matching architecture built on a 0.5B-parameter Llama transformer backbone.

## Prosody & Emotion Control

A key differentiator is emotion control. Chatterbox's exaggeration parameter (0-to-1) handles the tone: 0.3 for factual briefings, 0.5 for conversation, 0.7+ for storytelling. Chatterbox is the first open-source TTS model with emotion exaggeration control — dial expressiveness from monotone to dramatic with a single parameter. Meanwhile, XTTS-v2 does not rely on fine-grain prosody control but a reference encoder that learns to transfer acoustic and stylistic features automatically.

## Performance Comparison

Chatterbox scores 95/100 in quality comparisons while XTTS-v2 scores 75/100. Chatterbox's text-to-speech technology wins independent blind tests - 63% of listeners prefer the voice cloning over ElevenLabs for naturalness and clarity.

## Project Status

XTTS-v2 is built-in REST server with 44k GitHub stars, but the project has been dormant since Coqui shut down in 2024, while Chatterbox benefits from Resemble AI's commercial backing, providing unusual long-term stability guarantees.

Links: [{"title":"coqui/XTTS-v2 · Hugging Face","url":"https://huggingface.co/coqui/XTTS-v2"},{"title":"Coqui TTS (XTTS-v2): Local Voice Cloning Setup Guide | Local AI Master","url":"https://localaimaster.com/models/coqui-tts"},{"title":"Text-to-Speech Models 2026: TTS Leaderboard, Kokoro, XTTS and Voice Agents | CodeSOTA | CodeSOTA","url":"https://www.codesota.com/text-to-speech"},{"title":"Best Self-Hosted TTS: Open Source vs. On-Premise Voice ...","url":"https://inworld.ai/resources/best-self-hosted-tts"},{"title":"ⓍTTS - TTS 0.22.0 documentation","url":"https://docs.coqui.ai/en/latest/models/xtts.html"},{"title":"Self-Host AI Voice Cloning on GPU Cloud: XTTS-2, F5-TTS, and OpenVoice V2 Production Deployment Guide (2026) | Spheron Blog","url":"https://www.spheron.network/blog/self-host-voice-cloning-gpu-cloud-xtts-f5-tts-openvoice-v2/"},{"title":"Best Open Source TTS Models in 2026: Kokoro, Chatterbox, Fish Audio Compared","url":"https://www.tryspeakeasy.io/blog/open-source-text-to-speech-2026"},{"title":"Chatterbox: The Leading Family of Open-Source AI Voice Models That’s Changing the Game","url":"https://medium.com/@vinodpolinati/chatterbox-the-leading-family-of-open-source-ai-voice-models-thats-changing-the-game-150991d5cf8d"}]

I found comprehensive information about open-source TTS models with voice cloning and multi-speaker capabilities. Here's what the search revealed:

## Key Models Mentioned

**XTTS-v2:** A free, open-source voice-cloning and text-to-speech engine that runs locally on your machine. XTTS-v2 clones any voice from a short ~6-second sample and speaks 17 languages. It supports 17 languages and can clone a voice across languages: you can clone an English speaker and synthesize French output with that speaker's vocal characteristics.

**Chatterbox:** Chatterbox-Turbo (350M parameters, MIT license, sub-200ms with tuning, emotion control, voice cloning). In blind tests, Chatterbox hit a 63.75% preference rate against ElevenLabs. That's not a tie — that's a win.

**Fish-Speech:** Fish Speech 1.5 is the open-source alternative for CJK-heavy deployments. For multilingual voice cloning: Fish Audio S2-Pro.

**Zonos:** Zonos emotion-vector experiments are mentioned in controllability research, focusing on emotion and prosody control.

**Multi-Speaker & Narrator Models:** VibeVoice does something most TTS models can't: generate up to 90 minutes of continuous, multi-speaker audio. Four distinct speakers, stable voice identities, natural turn-taking.

## Key Features

- **Voice Cloning:** Multiple models support adding custom voices
- **Prosody & Rate Control:** The model maintains high intelligibility, natural prosody, and emotional expressiveness while being completely free and self-hostable
- **Multi-Speaker Support:** Available through models like VibeVoice and Dia2
- **Quality:** As of 2026 the quality gap is nearly closed: the best open-source TTS (Sesame CSM, 4.7 MOS) is within 0.1–0.3 of the top commercial API


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Expressive, long-form, multi-speaker conversational audio · GitHub","url":"https://github.com/wildminder/ComfyUI-VibeVoice"},{"title":"Microsoft VibeVoice: how ultra-low frame rate tokenization solved 90-minute multi-speaker TTS - Arun Baby","url":"https://www.arunbaby.com/speech-tech/0070-vibevoice-multi-speaker-long-form-tts/"}]

Based on the search results, here's comprehensive information about VibeVoice:

## Overview

VibeVoice is Microsoft's open-source text-to-speech (TTS) model purpose-built for multi-speaker, long-form, conversation-style audio. It can generate up to ~90 minutes of natural, turn-taking dialogue with up to four speakers, making it ideal for podcasts, audiobooks, and e-learning narration.

## Key Technical Features

Powered by continuous speech tokenizers (~7.5 Hz) and a next-token diffusion decoder, VibeVoice maintains strong speaker consistency and natural prosody over long sequences. VibeVoice employs a next-token diffusion framework, leveraging a Large Language Model (LLM) to understand textual context and dialogue flow, and a diffusion head to generate high-fidelity acoustic details.

## Voice Cloning

Clone any voice with just 10-60 seconds of audio sample, maintaining high fidelity and natural expression.

## License

The project is MIT-licensed, so you can run it locally or try it via hosted demos. However, there are important restrictions: The VibeVoice model is limited to research purpose use exploring highly realistic audio dialogue generation. This release is not intended or licensed for voice impersonation without explicit, recorded consent – cloning a real individual's voice for satire, advertising, ransom, social-engineering, or authentication bypass.

## Language Support

Primarily English and Chinese.

Based on the search results, here's comprehensive information about Zonos TTS:

## Overview

Zonos by Zyphra is a 0.4B-parameter open-weight text-to-speech model trained on 200K+ hours of multilingual speech. It is distributed under Apache 2.0, allowing unrestricted commercial and academic use.

## Key Features

**Voice Cloning & Local Inference:**
It performs zero-shot voice cloning from just 2–30 seconds of reference audio and offers fine-grained control over emotion, speaking rate, pitch variation, and audio quality. It achieves approximately 2× real-time inference speed on high-end GPUs (e.g., RTX 4090).

**Prosody Control:**
Zonos allows you to adjust speaking rate, pitch variation, and even audio quality. Zonos accepts an 8-dimensional emotion vector: [happiness, sadness, disgust, fear, surprise, anger, other, neutral].

**Emotion Control:**
The model lets you inject emotions like happiness, sadness, fear, and anger into the generated speech.

**Audio Quality:**
Output is high-fidelity 44 kHz audio.

## Model Variants

Two model variants are available: Transformer (best quality) and Hybrid/Mamba (faster inference).

## Local Setup

Zonos includes a Gradio WebUI, which offers an intuitive way to interact with Zonos, experiment with parameters, and generate speech with voice cloning and emotion control.

content.text: I have strong coverage across the key voice-control candidates (Chatterbox, XTTS-v2, VibeVoice, Zonos, Fish-Speech). Let me confirm Chatterbox's Apple Silicon / local practicality and rate control detail to round out the angle.

input.prompt: What voice cloning, prosody/emotion control, and speaking-rate control parameters does Chatterbox support? Does it support multi-speaker or two-speaker dialogue? What is the license, model size, and does it run on Apple Silicon / MPS / CPU? Does it emit any word or phoneme timestamps/alignment?

content.content:
# Chatterbox TTS: Feature and Technical Summary

**Voice Cloning & Prosody Control:**
The documentation mentions "voice cloning" via audio prompts and parameters like `cfg_weight` and `exaggeration`. For the multilingual model, you can "specify the audio prompt" for different voices. Turbo supports "Paralinguistic Tags (`[laugh]`)" for expressiveness, but the page doesn't detail phoneme-level prosody controls or speaking-rate parameters beyond general guidance.

**Multi-Speaker Dialogue:**
No multi-speaker or two-speaker dialogue functionality is documented.

**License & Model Size:**
"MIT license" is listed. Model sizes: Chatterbox-Turbo is 350M parameters; Chatterbox and Multilingual V3 are both 500M.

**Apple Silicon/MPS/CPU Support:**
The code shows `device = "cuda"  # or "cpu" / "mps"`, confirming MPS (Apple Silicon) and CPU compatibility.

**Timestamps/Alignment:**
No word or phoneme timestamps or alignment information is mentioned in the documentation.

content.text: I have comprehensive coverage of the voice-control angle. Let me compile the top results.

results.title: resemble-ai/chatterbox (GitHub) — SoTA open-source TTS with emotion-exaggeration control
results.snippet: Most relevant to the multi-voice/control angle. Chatterbox (MIT license; Turbo 350M, base/multilingual 500M) is the first open-source TTS with an 'exaggeration' (0-1) emotion/prosody dial plus 'cfg_weight' for pacing, and zero-shot voice cloning from a short audio prompt — ideal for distinguishing a narrator vs question voice by reference clip. Code explicitly supports device='mps' (Apple Silicon) and 'cpu', so it swaps into a Mac Kokoro pipeline. Caveats for this pipeline: no native multi-speaker dialogue mode (you'd render each speaker separately by reference audio) and no word/phoneme timestamps emitted (would need external forced alignment). Blind-test naturalness claims (63% preference vs ElevenLabs) are vendor-adjacent, so treat as marketing rather than independent MOS.

results.title: coqui/XTTS-v2 — Hugging Face model card (voice cloning baseline)
results.snippet: Direct cloning baseline to compare against. XTTS-v2 clones a voice from a ~6s sample across 17 languages (including EN, with cross-lingual transfer), and exposes speed/temperature controls. Relevant nuance for prosody: unlike Chatterbox/Zonos it has no explicit emotion-vector or exaggeration knob — it transfers style automatically via a reference encoder, giving less fine-grained control of a 'question voice' persona. Important practical flag: the Coqui-license model and project are effectively unmaintained since Coqui shut down in 2024, which matters for long-term pipeline stability. Runs locally (CPU/GPU), good for adding custom voices but weaker controllability than newer 2025-26 models.

results.title: Zyphra/Zonos — open-weight TTS with fine-grained prosody and emotion-vector control
results.snippet: Best fit for explicit prosody/rate control among the candidates. Zonos-v0.1 (0.4B, Apache-2.0 — fully commercial/redistribution OK, the most permissive license here) does zero-shot cloning from 2-30s reference and exposes direct controls for speaking rate, pitch variation, audio quality, plus an 8-dim emotion vector [happiness, sadness, disgust, fear, surprise, anger, other, neutral]. Outputs 44kHz. This is the strongest knob-level control for shaping a narrator vs question voice. Local-inference caveat: ~2x real-time is cited on an RTX 4090 (GPU-oriented); Apple Silicon/MPS viability and CPU speed are the open question for this Mac pipeline. No native word/phoneme timestamp output.

results.title: microsoft/VibeVoice — open-source long-form, multi-speaker conversational TTS
results.snippet: The only candidate with a true native two/multi-speaker mode, directly matching the narrator+question two-voice format: generates up to ~90 min of turn-taking dialogue with up to 4 stable speaker identities, voice-cloned from 10-60s samples (EN + Chinese). Built on an LLM + next-token-diffusion decoder for cross-turn prosody consistency. Critical licensing flag for an educational-podcast product: code is MIT but the model card restricts use to research only and forbids voice impersonation without recorded consent — a potential blocker for a published pipeline. Larger/heavier (1.5B) and GPU-oriented, so Apple Silicon practicality needs verification.

results.title: Best Open Source TTS Models in 2026: Kokoro, Chatterbox, Fish Audio Compared
results.snippet: Useful 2026 cross-model comparison framing the candidates against the Kokoro baseline on voice variety, cloning, and quality. Notes Fish-Speech/Fish-Audio as a strong multilingual voice-cloning option and positions Chatterbox as the leading controllable open model, while noting the open-vs-commercial quality gap is now ~0.1-0.3 MOS. A secondary/aggregator source — good for orientation and shortlisting, but its scores read more editorial than independent benchmark, so verify specific numbers against primary sources.

results.title: Zyphra debuts Zonos, an open-source TTS suite with expressive voice cloning
results.snippet: Independent coverage corroborating Zonos's controllability claims (expressive cloning, emotion/prosody control, Apache-2.0). Helps separate vendor marketing from third-party reporting on the rate/pitch/emotion knobs that make Zonos attractive for shaping two distinct podcast voices. Supplementary to the primary GitHub source but adds outside confirmation of the licensing and control-surface claims.

content.text:
I've completed the research on the voice-control angle (cloning, prosody, multi-speaker) and returned the structured output. Summary of findings:

**Top candidates for the narrator+question two-voice format:**

1. **Chatterbox** (MIT, 350M-500M, runs on MPS/CPU) — First open model with an emotion "exaggeration" knob + cloning; best controllability that confirms Apple Silicon support. No native multi-speaker mode or timestamps.

2. **XTTS-v2** (Coqui license, ~6s cloning) — The cloning baseline, but unmaintained since 2024 and weaker fine-grained control.

3. **Zonos** (Apache-2.0 — most permissive, 0.4B) — Best explicit prosody knobs (speaking rate, pitch variation, 8-dim emotion vector), 44kHz. GPU-oriented; Mac/MPS viability is the open question.

4. **VibeVoice** (MIT code but research-only model license) — The only one with a true native two/multi-speaker turn-taking mode, but its research-only/anti-impersonation license is a potential blocker for a published product.

Key flags I surfaced: none of these emit native word/phoneme timestamps (forced alignment needed separately), VibeVoice's licensing restriction, XTTS-v2's abandonment, and that Chatterbox's "63% vs ElevenLabs" naturalness claim is vendor-adjacent rather than independent MOS.
